Research article

Effect of user resistance on the organizational adoption of extended reality technologies: A mixed methods study
==================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Henri Jalo 1, Henri Pirkkalainen 2

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijinfomgt.2023.102731](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijinfomgt.2023.102731 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0268401223001123&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Examines organizational XR adoption using a mixed-methods approach. 
*   •Expected user resistance and perceived value were proposed to affect adoption. 
*   •Research model was tested using [structural equation modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/structural-equation-modeling "Learn more about structural equation modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approach (n=206). 
*   •Expected user resistance negatively affected perceived value and adoption intention. 
*   •12 conditions affecting model relationships were identified in a qualitative study. 

Abstract
--------

Although organizations are increasingly adopting [extended reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about extended reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR) technologies to transform their operations, their adoption has been slower than initially expected. Scholars and practitioners have pointed to user resistance as a potential hindrance to their adoption. This mixed-methods study examines how managerial perceptions of expected employee resistance and organizational value are related to organizational adoption intention. An organizational-level resistance-value [adoption model](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/adoption-model "Learn more about adoption model from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was developed and tested using the [structural equation modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/structural-equation-modeling "Learn more about structural equation modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approach with cross-sectional data from 206 European industrial [decision makers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/decision-maker "Learn more about decision makers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The results show that trialability, internal support capabilities, and extant user skills are negatively associated with expected employee resistance. Moreover, mimetic pressures strongly influence value perceptions, whereas expected employee resistance is negatively related to organizational value perceptions and adoption intentions. Perceived value had the strongest [positive relationship](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/positive-relationship "Learn more about positive relationship from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with adoption intention. A [qualitative study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/qualitative-research "Learn more about qualitative study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") consisting of 58 interviews further revealed 12 key conditions affecting the relationships between resistance, value, and adoption intention. As a theoretical contribution, we proposed and validated an integrated resistance-value adoption model that extends our understanding of factors affecting organizational XR adoption. As a practical contribution, practitioners can use the study’s results to help them focus on relevant factors for mitigating user resistance and promoting XR adoption.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001135)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S026840122300110X)

Keywords
--------

Augmented reality

Virtual reality

Extended reality

Organizational adoption

User resistance

1. Introduction
---------------

[Extended reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about Extended reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR) technologies, which include [augmented reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about augmented reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AR) and virtual reality (VR), are increasingly being adopted by organizations to bolster their competitiveness and respond to the new demands of digital and [remote work](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/remote-work "Learn more about remote work from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Chuah, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib14), [Dwivedi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib30), [Dwivedi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib31), [Masood and Egger, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib77)). Recent examples include Accenture acquiring 60,000 VR headsets to train their workers ([Fink, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib34)) and Mercedes-Benz USA training 1200 of their dealership technicians to use AR headsets in remote maintenance ([Castellanos, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib10)). Although XR technologies have remarkable potential in facilitating collaboration and displaying information to organizational users in novel and effective ways ([Hennig-Thurau et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib47)), such as reviewing designs remotely in VR rather than using physical mockups ([Wolfartsberger, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib129)), their adoption has been slower than many [industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analysts and researchers initially expected ([Chuah, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib14), [Grand View Research, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib43)). As XR technologies can be used to radically transform organizational activities throughout the organizational value chain ([Dwivedi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib30); [Porter & Heppelmann, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib92); [Torro et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib120)), these kinds of considerable changes can also create broader resistance to adopting such new ways of working ([Chuah, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib14), [Kim and Kankanhalli, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib64)). While initial research on XR adoption has identified users’ resistance to change as an important barrier ([Badamasi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib3), [Davila Delgado et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib20)), there is still a dearth of research on the role of user resistance in organizational adoption dynamics. This article aims to address this research gap.

Although the importance of user resistance in organizational technology adoption failures has been widely noted by scholars (e.g., [Ali et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib1); [Kim & Kankanhalli, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib64); [Laumer & Eckhardt, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib68)), much of this research has focused on [individual users](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/individual-user "Learn more about individual users from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (e.g., [Kim & Kankanhalli, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib64); [Laumer et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib69)). In contrast, the role of user resistance in organizational adoption dynamics has received less attention. However, as central drivers of organizational change ([Vagnani & Volpe, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib122)), managers’ beliefs about the potential level of employee resistance to adopting a technology are likely to have a significant influence on whether it is implemented in the organization ([Jeyaraj et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib57), [Markus, 1983](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib74)). Thus, decision makers’ value assessments of novel technologies are likely to be highly dependent on their expectations of their employees’ responses to their implementation. Due to the wide [application potential](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/potential-application "Learn more about application potential from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of XR ([Chuah, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib14); [Porter & Heppelmann, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib92)), it is important to holistically consider how expected resistance is related to adoption at the aggregate organizational level. Therefore, understanding the interplay between expected employee resistance and perceived organizational value can yield important insights into critical aspects that affect organizational adoption of XR. _The main objective of this study is to examine how decision makers’ perceptions of the expected level of employee resistance to XR and the value of XR are related to organizational adoption and what conditions amplify or mitigate these relationships._

A mixed-methods approach was used in the study. In the first [quantitative study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/quantitative-study "Learn more about quantitative study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), we drew on the organizational-level value-risk [adoption model](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/adoption-model "Learn more about adoption model from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") presented by [Gao et al. (2012)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib38) and conceptualized expected employee resistance as a specific adoption risk. The proposed research model incorporating the effects of these two proximate antecedents of organizational adoption intention, namely, expected employee resistance to XR (as perceived by managers) and the perceived organizational value of XR, was then extended with the technology–organization–environment (TOE) framework ([DePietro et al., 1990](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib21)) to ascertain which distal antecedents enhance value perceptions or diminish the expected level of resistance from employees. The research model was tested using the [structural equation modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/structural-equation-modeling "Learn more about structural equation modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SEM) approach with cross-sectional survey data from 206 managers and [decision makers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/decision-maker "Learn more about decision makers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from European industrial companies. In addition, a second [qualitative study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/qualitative-research "Learn more about qualitative study from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was conducted based on 58 interviews with European industrial managers to provide a richer understanding of the dynamics and conditions affecting the relationships between the three key variables of the study (i.e., resistance, value, and adoption intention).

The present study contributes to [information systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/information-system "Learn more about information systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IS) research by theorizing and validating a research model that integrates user resistance and value perspectives, examines their relationships with organizational XR adoption intention, and specifies under which conditions these relationships are amplified or mitigated. This contribution enhances our understanding of the dynamics that affect the adoption of emerging technologies, such as XR, wherein the role of resistance is likely to be more prominent than for established technologies. Furthermore, this study provides additional contextualization to earlier qualitative findings at the organizational level of analysis (e.g., [Lapointe & Rivard, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib67)) by quantitatively validating the significance of the user resistance construct and its role in organizational adoption considerations. The study also has practical implications, as the validated model can help managers focus their efforts on factors that are critical in mitigating resistance and promoting XR adoption.

The rest of the paper consists of the following sections. [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#sec0010) examines the theoretical background of XR and user resistance in organizations. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#sec0035) describes the mixed-methods study design. [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#sec0040) presents the research model and hypotheses for Study 1, along with the survey design, data collection methods, and the results of the [quantitative data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/quantitative-data "Learn more about quantitative data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analysis. [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#sec0115) describes the qualitative data collection methods, analysis techniques, and findings for Study 2. Finally, in [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#sec0155), the results and findings are discussed along with the theoretical and practical contributions of the study. We conclude with the study’s limitations and suggestions for future research.

2. Theoretical background
-------------------------

### 2.1. AR and VR in organizational use

[AR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about AR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be defined as a technology that _combines or superimposes digital elements into the user’s view of the real world with handheld devices, such as smartphones and tablets, or with head-mounted displays (HMDs)_ ([Azuma, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib2), [Masood and Egger, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib76)). Although [AR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/augmented-reality "Learn more about AR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") HMDs offer superior interactions, freedom of movement, and better tracking of the environment, smartphones and tablets are still the most common way for users to utilize AR, as they are far more accessible than AR HMDs ([Billinghurst, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib7)). In contrast, VR can be defined as a technology that _transports the user into an immersive and interactive 3D environment with a_ _[spatial presence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/spatial-presence "Learn more about spatial presence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ ([Bryson, 1995](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib8), [Shu et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib107)). In particular, recent developments in HMD-based [immersive VR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/immersive-virtual-reality "Learn more about immersive VR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been remarkable ([Hennig-Thurau et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib47), [Torro et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib120)), which was also the focus of our study.

AR and VR are often examined together, as they enable users to interact with digital information in more immersive and interactive ways (e.g., [Davila Delgado et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib20); [Noghabaei et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib83); [Steffen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib112)). Recent research also suggests that awareness, perceived adoption limitations, and the actual use of AR and VR are at similar levels in organizations ([Jalo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib56)). Many [industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leaders also believe that AR and VR are converging, with both technologies being increasingly integrated into single hardware offerings ([Robertson, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib99), [Stein, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib113)). Researchers and practitioners have recently referred to AR and VR using the umbrella term XR ([Chuah, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib14), [Dwivedi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib29), [Dwivedi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib30)). This study also examines these two technologies simultaneously under the XR label.

In addition to their use in the consumer context, XR technologies have found many uses in industry ([Lounakoski et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib71)). Industrial XR use cases include, for instance, remote immersive design reviews ([Devanesan, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib22)), training and onboarding of workers ([Fink, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib34)), marketing and sales ([Lounakoski et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib71)), and remote operational support ([Porter & Heppelmann, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib92)). Examples from industry indicate that XR offers substantial performance and quality improvements. For instance, Kia Motors went from spending several days on face-to-face physical design reviews to completing remote XR reviews of designs within a few hours ([Devanesan, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib22)). Productivity improvements of 25 % or more are often reported in various manufacturing activities ([Porter & Heppelmann, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib92)). As organizational value appropriation examples begin to mount, organizations are increasingly considering the adoption of XR technologies to enhance the efficiency of their operations and to maintain their competitiveness ([Dwivedi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib30), [Dwivedi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib31), [Jalo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib56)).

Previous research has identified several critical factors for organizational XR adoption. A review of the extant literature can be found in Appendix A (Table A1). Some of the key identified factors include the maturity of XR hardware ([Davila Delgado et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib20), [Masood and Egger, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib76)), [IS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/information-system "Learn more about IS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") compatibility with XR ([Jalo et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib55), [Jalo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib56), [Masood and Egger, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib76)), [aversion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/aversion "Learn more about aversion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to change ([Badamasi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib3)), user acceptance and inertia ([Berg and Vance, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib5), [Masood and Egger, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib76), [Masood and Egger, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib77)), XR expertise availability ([Badamasi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib3), [Chandra and Kumar, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib11), [Davila Delgado et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0268401223001123#bib20)), the existence of a champion ([Berg & Vanc

SUMMARY:
To be clear, Joshi – like many other people in the tech industry – believes there absolutely are use cases for AR/VR in the enterprise. _We’re using AR/VR here as an umbrella term that could refer to a growing variety of hardware and software technologies, including new-ish jargon such as “extended reality,” “mixed reality,” “metaverse,” and the like. 1.   **Competing technology priorities:** Here’s another reality that will push AR/VR down some strategic IT priority lists: AR/VR development software isn’t the hot new tech anymore, and it’s getting squeezed out by bigger stars, including the biggest of them all at the moment. AR/VR in the Enterprise: All Smoke and Mirrors?
===============================================

[![Image 2: Kevin Casey](https://thectoclub.com/wp-content/cache/thectoclub.com/static/secure.gravatar.com/d0c0ddb0fc0c2a95f480b4f1e914c6091c327d14)](https://thectoclub.com/author/kevincasey/)

 By 

Kevin Casey

[](https://thectoclub.com/author/kevincasey/)

Kevin Casey

Kevin Casey is an award-winning technology and business writer with deep expertise in digital media. He covers all things IT, with a particular interest in cloud computing, software development, security, careers, leadership, and culture. Kevin's stories have been mentioned in The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, CIO Journal, and other publications. His InformationWeek.com on ageism in the tech industry, "Are You Too Old For IT?," won an Azbee Award from the American Society of Business Publication Editors (ASBPE), and he's a former Community Choice honoree in the Small Business Influencer Awards. In the corporate world, he's worked for startups and Fortune 500 firms – as well as with their partners and customers – to develop content driven by business goals and customer needs. He can turn almost any subject matter into stories that connect with their intended audience, and has done so for companies like Red Hat, Verizon, New Relic, Puppet Labs, Intuit, American Express, HPE, Dell, and others. Kevin teaches writing at Duke University, where he is a Lecturing Fellow in the nationally recognized Thompson Writing Program.

[More about Kevin](https://thectoclub.com/author/kevincasey/)

 Last updated on Jul 16, 2025 

 Discover how to deliver better software and systems in rapidly scaling environments.[Join our Newsletter](https://thectoclub.com/subscribe/)

QUICK SUMMARY
AR/VR promises immersive business solutions but faces real-world hurdles such as cost, limited adoption, and competition from AI. This article separates the hype from the reality, providing expert insights to help you decide if AR/VR is right for your organization.

TABLE OF CONTENTS[Where Things Stand Today](https://thectoclub.com/strategy-innovation/ar-vr-in-the-enterprise/#h-where-things-stand-today)[AR/VR for Business: 4 Big Challenges](https://thectoclub.com/strategy-innovation/ar-vr-in-the-enterprise/#h-ar-vr-for-business-4-big-challenges)[On the Horizon: New Business Cases for AR/VR](https://thectoclub.com/strategy-innovation/ar-vr-in-the-enterprise/#h-on-the-horizon-new-business-cases-for-ar-vr)

![Image 3: AR VR in the Enterprise featured image](https://thectoclub.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/13/2025/01/CTO-AR-VR-in-the-Enterprise-featured-image-792x446.jpg)

Key Takeaways

**AR/VR: Promises vs. Reality:**Despite the initial hype around AR/VR technologies, many believe they have yet to meet their expected impact, raising questions about their true potential in business.

**Tried-and-True Use Cases:**Yugal Joshi highlights practical applications like employee training and remote support, which demonstrate AR/VR's value in enterprise settings through cost-effective solutions.

**Consumer Tech: A Mixed Bag:**AR/VR headset shipments saw a decline in early 2024, but a significant predicted growth suggests a complex consumer landscape that still holds promise.

**Workplace Wonders or Tech Mirage?:**While AR/VR can enhance business operations, the vision of widespread adoption in offices remains uncertain, challenging the notion of its seamless integration into daily tasks.

**Growth Ahead (with Caution):**Forecasts suggest strong growth for AR/VR technologies from 2024-2028, yet skepticism about their full potential continues to temper excitement among businesses and consumers alike.

We were promised a metaverse; all we got was this lousy headset.

That’s not much of a t-shirt slogan, much less an endorsement of the state of virtual and [augmented reality](https://thectoclub.com/tools/best-augmented-reality-software/) (VR/AR). Still, it might capture the general sentiment about AR/VR today – especially when considering potential business uses for the technology.

_“There is a general feeling that AR/VR has not made the intended impact compared to its potential,”_ says Yugal Joshi, partner at the technology research firm [Everest Group](https://www.everestgrp.com/), where he leads the firm’s cloud and digital services research, as well as advising clients on emerging technology adoption.

In this article, I’ll do my best to cut through the noise and get a clearer sense of the opportunities and challenges so you can make a more informed decision.

Where Things Stand Today
------------------------

To be clear, Joshi – like many other people in the tech industry – believes there absolutely are use cases for AR/VR in the enterprise. Joshi notes some “tried and true” examples like employee training, advanced design and prototyping, remote support, collaboration, and customer experience personalization.

_“These have been tried and adopted across a variety of enterprises,”_ Joshi shares, adding that cost reductions have been a standard business justification. An industrial firm can train staff on heavy machinery without having to invest in or risk expensive industrial assets, for example.

In the consumer world, meanwhile. AR/VR headsets and other technologies are a growing concern. While headset shipments actually declined in the first half of 2024, research firm IDC reported a [12.8% increase](https://www.idc.com/getdoc.jsp?containerId=prUS52368924) in Q3. The company predicts a 43.9% compound annual growth rate in headset shipments from 2024-2028.

![Image 4: Author's Note](https://thectoclub.com/wp-content/cache/thectoclub.com/static/secure.gravatar.com/8e4f02aa65339339eb19269849680b74586e8ab6)

Author's Note

_We’re using AR/VR here as an umbrella term that could refer to a growing variety of hardware and [software](https://thectoclub.com/tools/best-virtual-reality-software/) technologies, including new-ish jargon such as “extended reality,” “mixed reality,” “metaverse,” and the like. Purists beware._

So we’re all forgiven for believing the initial hype: AR/VR at least _seems_ like useful tech in business environments and a potentially profitable one at that. Yet the image of an office full of people zooming purposefully around in AR/VR headsets seems far-fetched, to put it gently.

What’s the real story? Is AR/VR in the enterprise fact or fiction? Like many worthwhile questions, the answer isn’t straightforward.

Whereas some hot and emergent tech – AI, anyone? – arrives with potential impacts and use cases for virtually any business, AR/VR’s viability depends significantly on the specific needs and goals of your organization.

AR/VR for Business: 4 Big Challenges
------------------------------------

Again, some of the hype around [AR/VR](https://thectoclub.com/tools/best-virtual-reality-development-software/) is justified. If a retailer can elegantly enable customers to “try on” clothing or accessories via an immersive digital experience – without setting foot in an actual store or dressing room – that could be a boon for sales (and reducing online returns). Similar experiences abound: what if you “test-drove” your next car from your couch?

For some companies, significant investments in AR/VR tech may not make any sense – there’s not enough of a business case to justify the costs and maintenance.

Regardless, some headwinds are blowing currently that may chill short-term adoption in business environments. In no particular order:

1.   **Return-to-office (RTO) mandates:** Over the past five or so years, there has been a major shift toward remote and hybrid office models. Now, there appears to be a potent push—at least among some employers—for people to return to the physical office. That cycle is a potential problem for AR/VR adoption in the enterprise. 

_“Many companies have accelerated their return to work initiatives,”_ Joshi says. _“Given hybrid work was an important drive for AR/VR, there is a potential challenge toward its adoption in this use case.”_

1.   **Competing technology priorities:** Here’s another reality that will push AR/VR down some strategic IT priority lists: AR/[VR development software](https://thectoclub.com/tools/best-vr-development-software/) isn’t the hot new tech anymore, and it’s getting squeezed out by bigger stars, including the biggest of them all at the moment.

_“AI adoption has crowded other initiatives in enterprises, and AR/VR is one of those,”_ remarks Joshi. _“Therefore, the technology must wrestle hard to get leadership attention.”_

According to Joshi, that's compounded by stagnation in the visible enterprise use cases. The ones we covered above are roughly the same as the ones people were talking about when AR/VR first arrived on the scene.

_"AI-first vendors are [pushing the envelope of innovation](https://thectoclub.com/career/best-innovation-conferences/) and bringing newer use cases, whereas the AR/VR industry is stuck where it was many years ago,”_ declares Joshi.

1.   **Costs may nullify cost****_savings_****:** Business and technology leaders need business reasons to justify new tech investments. This _could_ be a significant driver in enterprise AR/VR adoption, but only if the costs eventually come down.

_“Most companies want to trim their R&D costs given the macro situation. AR/VR could be a strong lever to achieve this objective,”_ Joshi comments. _“However, given the large upfront investments, it becomes hard to justify the business case.”_

Apple’s Vision Pro headset starts at [$3,499](https://www.apple.com/shop/buy-vision/apple-vision-pro), for example. How eager are you to outfit your whole team with one?

1.   **The usual enterprise suspects:**The story of AR/VR to this point is largely a consumer narrative. The reality is that the biggest opportunities at the moment are in consumer spaces: gaming (duh) and other forms of entertainment, retail, and so forth.

This begs the question: do you really want one of your team members using your corporate apps or accessing your data with a consumer entertainment device?

_“Issues around interoperability, security, and privacy of data collected are getting more attention from enterprises,”_ observes Joshi. Other familiar forms of user resistance also apply: bulky devices, cultural resistance, connectivity, and other performance issues.

10 CTO Club Picks for Top VR Engines!
-------------------------------------

Here's my pick of the 10 best software from the 10 tools reviewed.

1.   1. [Windows Mixed Reality](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/mixed-reality/windows-mixed-reality) — [Best for hybrid reality experiences on Windows](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/mixed-reality/windows-mixed-reality) 
2.   2. [Amazon Lumberyard](https://aws.amazon.com/lumberyard/) — [Best for AWS-integrated game development](https://aws.amazon.com/lumberyard/) 
3.   3. [UPBGE](https://upbge.org/#/) — [Best for Blender game engine enthusiasts](https://upbge.org/#/) 
4.   4. [Unity Engine](https://unity.com/products/unity-engine) — [Best for multiplatform game developments](https://unity.com/products/unity-engine) 
5.   5. [ARCore](https://developers.google.com/ar) — [Best for Android augmented reality experiences](https://developers.google.com/ar) 
6.   6. [SteamVR](https://www.steamvr.com/en/) — [Best for integration with Steam's gaming ecosystem](https://www.steamvr.com/en/) 
7.   7. [Unreal Engine](https://www.unrealengine.com/en-US/unreal-engine-5) — [Best for high-fidelity, interactive experiences](https://www.unrealengine.com/en-US/unreal-engine-5) 
8.   8. [VIVE Wave](https://developer.vive.com/eu/wave/) — [Best for cross-platform virtual reality developments](https://developer.vive.com/eu/wave/) 
9.   9. [VirBELA](https://www.virbela.com/) — [Best for collaborative virtual spaces](https://www.virbela.com/) 
10.   10. [Conical Interactive Studios](https://www.conical.nz/interactive-studios/) — [Best for bespoke VR and AR solutions](https://www.conical.nz/interactive-studios/) 

 Show More (5) 

![Image 5](https://impression.crozdesk.com/tracker/v2/p/cto/c/15421/111716-86836-111711-111718-111714-111717-87323-111712-111710-111713/impression.png?r=aHR0cHM6Ly90aGVjdG9jbHViLmNvbS9zdHJhdGVneS1pbm5vdmF0aW9uL2FyLXZyLWluLXRoZS1lbnRlcnByaXNlLw%3D%3D&language=EN&wpp_id=cto_18122)![Image 6](https://crozdesk.com/tracker/v2/cto/c/15421/111716-86836-111711-111718-111714-111717-87323-111712-111710-111713/conversion.png?language=EN&wpp_id=cto_18122)

On the Horizon: New Business Cases for AR/VR
--------------------------------------------

So is AR/VR the holiday gift that no one really wanted, or the real deal?

It’s still nascent tech, and there are certainly macro trends globally that could drive greater adoption – such as continued improvements in mobile and fixed-line internet connections globally. If 10Gb connections became ubiquitous, for example, that would almost certainly spawn new generations of digital experiences.)

As the tech improves overall, the potential uses should, too. In the near term, here are three ideas about what could move AR/VR up enterprise IT priority lists.

1.   **Consumers demand it _en masse_.** If AR/VR hit some tipping point with consumers – not just, say, serious gamers, but virtually anyone – then some industries and businesses would probably need to pay attention. Virtually any consumer business – apparel, travel, entertainment, automotive, e-commerce – would have a greater interest in offering immersive customer experiences if those customers were spending significantly more time on AR/[VR devices](https://thectoclub.com/tools/best-virtual-reality-development-software/) at some point.

1.   **Vendors develop enterprise-grade solutions.** “Enterprise-grade solution” is the bane of any self-respecting tech writer, but that’s kind of the point: enterprise vendors know they need to invest seriously in the un-sexy features serious businesses require, all the more so if they hope to sell into highly regulated industries such as healthcare or financial services. Hardware and software that prioritizes security, governance, interoperability, and other requirements will be key to increased adoption. If you're considering implementing AR solutions, working with [specialized AR app development companies](https://thectoclub.com/services/best-ar-app-development-company/) can help navigate these enterprise requirements more effectively than adapting consumer-focused solutions.

_“Many vendors are coming with their APIs, SDKs, and interoperable platforms that can alleviate some of these concerns, but these are yet to be broadly adopted,”_ Joshi claims.

1.   **The tech gets better.** Last but not least, there’s ample room for improvement in the tech, starting with the hardware.

_“I expect a lot of development in ‘headless’ AR/VR systems,”_ Joshi posits. _“These need to get embedded in the day-to-day workings of enterprises rather than needing to always wear headsets."_

He points to examples like recent innovations in smart glasses as promising signs.

_“These can accelerate AR/VR adoption in enterprises,”_ according to Joshi. _“In addition, embedding [AR/VR experiences] in browser-based access – [such as] smart car showrooms – can also help adoption.”_

enriched_points.expanded_content: Many traditional game developers and industry professionals express skepticism about VR's long-term viability, fearing it may not deliver on its promises and remain a niche market.
High upfront costs for VR technology, coupled with uncertain ROI, deter many companies from making substantial investments, limiting broader industry adoption and innovation.
Organizations face significant training requirements to effectively implement VR, necessitating investment in developing skilled personnel who can operate and manage VR technologies.
The current VR market struggles with readiness, as many consumers and businesses remain unaware of the technology's capabilities, hindering widespread adoption and effective use.
Cultural perceptions of VR as primarily entertainment hinder its acceptance in professional settings, as businesses wrestle with integrating immersive experiences into their workflows.
